skill.name: generating-solana-projects
skill.description: Generates Solana dApp project scaffolds using Anchor 0.32.1 and Next.js, including Rust program templates, TypeScript tests, wallet setup, and deployment guidance.
skill.summary: Create Anchor-based Solana project scaffolds with tests and a Next.js wallet frontend.
skill.author: MSzgy

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, filesystem, network, and script patterns, but review shows most are markdown templates, Solana configuration examples, localhost endpoints, and relative imports. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious exfiltration was found. The remaining risk is moderate because the skill uses Write and Bash-capable workflows and instructs users to run build, install, deploy, and frontend commands.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled Project Generation Requires Path Guardrails
medium_findings.description: The skill asks for a project name and then generates a complete directory tree. This is legitimate for a scaffolding skill, but implementations should normalize project names and prevent writes outside the intended workspace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow clearly depends on user-provided project names and file creation. The files do not show malicious intent, so this is a moderate implementation risk rather than a confirmed exploit.

medium_findings.title: External Build And Deployment Commands Need User Review
medium_findings.description: The skill provides commands for dependency installation, Anchor builds, local validators, deployment, tests, and frontend servers. These commands are expected for Solana development, but users should review generated files before running them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly documented and are normal for this domain. Risk remains because install, build, and deploy commands execute external tooling in the user environment.

low_findings.title: Markdown Fences Misclassified As Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Many static external command findings are caused by markdown code fences and project tree examples. These are documentation templates, not Ruby or shell backtick execution in runnable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are fenced markdown examples and directory trees. There is no executable Ruby code or command substitution at these locations.

low_findings.title: Template Paths Are Legitimate Solana Project References
low_findings.description: Hidden directory and traversal findings refer to installation paths, Solana wallet configuration, and relative imports for generated clients. These are expected template references, not evidence of unauthorized file access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths appear in documented templates and import statements. No instruction directs the model to read secrets or traverse arbitrary user files.

low_findings.title: Network Indicators Are Expected Development Endpoints
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL and IP address are an Anchor registry URL and local Solana validator endpoint. They do not contain credential upload logic or suspicious remote collection behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed endpoints are standard development or registry locations. No evidence found that secrets or project data are sent to an untrusted service.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: Static weak cryptography findings correspond to ordinary documentation terms such as smart contracts, tests, descriptions, and frontend configuration. No weak hashing or encryption implementation was found in the reviewed locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are prose or placeholders, not cryptographic operations. No evidence found for MD5, SHA-1, insecure ciphers, or custom crypto logic.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import Alert Is A JSDoc Type Reference
low_findings.description: The dynamic import finding points to a Next.js JSDoc type annotation in a configuration template. This does not dynamically load untrusted code at runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The import syntax is inside a documentation comment used by Next.js tooling. There is no user-controlled module path or runtime import expression.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash And Write Capabilities For Scaffolding
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill declares Bash, Write, and Read access and is designed to create full project structures. This capability is expected, but marketplace users should treat it as a file-writing developer automation skill.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The capability declaration and workflow are explicit. The pattern is not malicious by itself, but it increases impact if prompts or generated paths are mishandled.

dangerous_patterns.title: Generated Deployment Workflow Executes External Tooling
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation instructs users to run package installation, Anchor build, validator, deploy, and test commands. These are normal commands for Solana projects but should run only after review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command workflow is directly present and domain appropriate. Risk depends on generated project contents and installed dependencies rather than on confirmed malicious code in the skill.

content.value_statement: Solana projects require coordinated Rust, Anchor, TypeScript, and wallet setup. This skill produces structured project scaffolds with tests, frontend integration, and deployment guidance.
actual_capabilities: Creates Anchor project structures for Solana dApps.
Generates Rust smart contract templates with state, errors, and instruction modules.
Adds TypeScript test scaffolds using the Anchor testing framework.
Creates Next.js frontend templates with Solana wallet adapter setup.
Provides setup steps for program IDs, builds, deployment, tests, and local frontend use.
limitations: Generated contracts still require security review before real funds are used.
The skill uses fixed dependency versions that may become outdated.
It does not deploy projects automatically without user action.
Project-specific business logic must be completed and tested by the developer.

use_cases.title: Prototype A Solana dApp
use_cases.description: Create a working project layout for a new Solana app with program, tests, and frontend files.

use_cases.title: Teach Anchor Project Structure
use_cases.description: Show learners how Rust programs, Anchor configuration, generated types, and wallet clients fit together.

use_cases.title: Standardize Internal Templates
use_cases.description: Generate consistent starter projects for teams building token, DAO, marketplace, escrow, or staking applications.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Basic Voting App
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Solana voting project named community-vote with poll creation, one vote per wallet, vote counts, tests, and a simple Next.js frontend.

prompt_templates.title: Generate A Token Program
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a Solana token management project with mint, transfer, freeze, and admin update instructions. Include Anchor tests and wallet UI components.

prompt_templates.title: Build A Marketplace Starter
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a Solana NFT marketplace scaffold with listing, offers, cancellation, purchase flow, account state, validation errors, tests, and frontend screens.

prompt_templates.title: Design A DeFi Protocol Skeleton
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Anchor 0.32.1 DeFi lending project skeleton with pool state, deposits, withdrawals, borrow flow, risk checks, tests, and Next.js wallet integration.

output_examples.input: Create a Solana escrow project for peer-to-peer token swaps.
output_examples.output: A complete Anchor workspace outline with escrow state, initialize, exchange, and cancel instructions, TypeScript tests, and a wallet-connected frontend plan.

output_examples.input: Generate a DAO governance starter with proposals and voting.
output_examples.output: A generated project plan covering DAO, proposal, and member accounts, instruction handlers, test scenarios, and governance portal components.

output_examples.input: Build a staking dashboard project with rewards.
output_examples.output: A staking project scaffold with pool and user stake accounts, reward claim flow, Anchor tests, and frontend dashboard pages.

best_practices: Review all generated smart contract logic before build or deployment.
Run tests on localnet before connecting generated code to devnet or mainnet.
Replace placeholder program IDs in every referenced file before deployment.
anti_patterns: Do not deploy generated contracts without a security review.
Do not store private keys or seed phrases in generated source files.
Do not accept unsanitized project names that write outside the workspace.

faq.question: Does this skill deploy Solana programs automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides project files and command guidance. The user must review and run deployment commands.

faq.question: Which Anchor version does it target?
faq.answer: It targets Anchor 0.32.1 and matching Rust and TypeScript package versions listed in the skill.

faq.question: Can it create a frontend?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a Next.js frontend template with Solana wallet adapter setup and Anchor client wiring.

faq.question: Are generated contracts production ready?
faq.answer: They are starter scaffolds. Production use requires testing, threat modeling, and a smart contract security review.

faq.question: What project types are supported?
faq.answer: The examples cover voting, NFT marketplaces, staking, DAOs, escrow services, tokens, and other Solana dApps.

faq.question: Does it require internet access?
faq.answer: The skill content itself is local, but generated projects usually need package installation and Solana development tooling.
